coredata.dc:title: Fluorometric carbon-dots nanosensor for the detection of hexavalent chromium in water
coredata.dc:creator: Hahn, Misun
Jang, Myeongseok
Cho, Youngseul
Bae, Minjun
Han, Min Young
Piao, Yuanzhe
coredata.dc:description:
The detection of trace residues of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) in water is crucial for assessing ecological hazards due to its high cytotoxicity and carcinogenic properties. In this study, we have developed a selective and sensitive fluorescent sensor utilizing carbon dots (CDs) with an average size of 2 nm. The CDs is synthesized via a low-cost and facile one-step method without the need for additional heteroatoms. The CDs-based sensor exhibits superior selectivity, even in the presence of environmentally relevant cations, and can detect Cr(VI) over a concentration range of 1–1000 μM (with a linear range of 1
–
125 μM and R2
≥
0.998). The detection limit of the sensor is found to be 0.6 μM (equivalent to 31 μg L−1), which is below the value of 50 μg L−1 for drinking water guide-lined by the World Health Organization. This performance can be attributed to the combination of inner filter effect with the static quenching effect between the as-synthesized CDs and Cr(VI). Furthermore, we demonstrate the applicability of the CDs-based sensor for Cr(VI) detection in real samples of drinking water and tap water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
Sensor
Hexavalent chromium
Cr(VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Test of pH and stabilized time of CDs
Fluorescent detection of Cr(VI) and interference cations
Detection of Cr(VI) in water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning
Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2023R1A2C2007699 and 2022R1A6A1A0306303912 ) and the Nano Material Technology Development Program through the NRF funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT ( NRF-2015M3A7B6027970 ). In addition, this research was partly supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning ( KETEP ) funded by the Korea government ( MOTIE ) ( 20215710100170 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (b) Size distribution (c) HR-TEM image and (d) XRD pattern of as-synthesized CDs.

(A) XPS wide spectrum, high-resolution XPS data of (b) N1s, (c) C1s, (d) O1s of as-obtained CDs, (e) IR transmittance spectra of CDs and precursors, and (f) UV–Vis. absorbance and PL emission at different excitation wavelengths of CDs.

(A) Images of CDs solutions after adding cations (1 mM, upper) and after spiking Cr(VI) (1 mM, lower) in the presence of other ions under 365 nm irradiation, (b) FL intensities ratio (F/F0, blank) of CDs solutions after adding cations (green bar) and after spiking Cr(VI) in the presence of other ions (red bar).

(A) Fitting curve of FL ratio for 1
−
1000 μM (the inset showing the linear range of 1
−
125 μM) (b) FL spectra of the CDs at 350 nm excitation for different Cr(VI) concentrations.
(A) UV–Vis. absorbance of Cr(VI) and FL spectra of CDs (b) FL decays of CDs before & after adding Cr(VI) at 365 nm excitation.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs and the sensing mechanism of Cr(VI).
Quantification of Cr(VI) in real water samples using as-prepared CDs and ICP-OES.
Comparison of the as-prepared CDs with different CDs for Cr(VI) detection.

head.ce:title: Fluorometric carbon-dots nanosensor for the detection of hexavalent chromium in water

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Convergence Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The detection of trace residues of hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) in water is crucial for assessing ecological hazards due to its high cytotoxicity and carcinogenic properties. In this study, we have developed a selective and sensitive fluorescent sensor utilizing carbon dots (CDs) with an average size of 2 nm. The CDs is synthesized via a low-cost and facile one-step method without the need for additional heteroatoms. The CDs-based sensor exhibits superior selectivity, even in the presence of environmentally relevant cations, and can detect Cr(VI) over a concentration range of 1–1000 μM (with a linear range of 1
–
125 μM and R2
≥
0.998). The detection limit of the sensor is found to be 0.6 μM (equivalent to 31 μg L−1), which is below the value of 50 μg L−1 for drinking water guide-lined by the World Health Organization. This performance can be attributed to the combination of inner filter effect with the static quenching effect between the as-synthesized CDs and Cr(VI). Furthermore, we demonstrate the applicability of the CDs-based sensor for Cr(VI) detection in real samples of drinking water and tap water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium, classified as a heavy metal, exhibits various oxidative states ranging from −2 to +6 with only the 0, +3, and +6 states remaining stable under environmental conditions [1]. Its biochemical characteristics also range from being a biologically inert compound as metallic chromium (Cr(0)) to an essential trace nutrient in the form of trivalent chromium (Cr(III)). Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) is associated with toxicity and carcinogenesis [2]. Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) compounds have been extensively utilized in various industries such as metal coating, electroplating, leather tanning, and the manufacturing of paints and dyes. Despite their practicality, Cr(VI) poses a significantly greater environmental risk and is more toxic and active to human and aquatic life compared to other chromium ions [1,2]. Consequently, the discharge of Cr(VI) into the aquatic environment is strictly regulated at lower concentration levels by many nations and the World Health Organization [1,3]. Over the past few decades, several methods have been developed to distinguish Cr(VI) from other similar ions and determine its content, including photometric methods [4], ion chromatography [5], and high performance liquid chromatography-inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (HPLC-ICP-MS) [1,6]. However, these methods suffer from various drawbacks such as higher instrument costs, time-consuming preparation, and complex analysis procedures [7,8].

In comparison, fluorescence detection of Cr(VI) has been reported as a promising alternative to complex methods utilizing commercial instrument systems due to its advantages of easy to handle, high sensitivity, and selectivity. Among the various materials, carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent probes or sensors exhibit various merits, including low cost, a facile synthesis route, lower toxicity, chemical stability, quick response, and tunable fluorescence characteristics [9,10]. These properties have enabled the consistent development of CDs-based fluorescent sensors for various ions (e.g., Fe3+ [11], Cu2+ [12], Au3+ [13], etc.) and specific molecular substances (e.g., glutathione [14], benzo[a]pyrene [15], H2O2 [16], etc.). However, the Cr(VI) detection using CDs has been reported in limited studies, often utilizing the hybrid effect through heteroatom co-doping (e.g., B, S, and metal elements) and multi-step synthesis [17–24].

Recently, we introduced fluorescent CDs that exhibit excellent performance through a low-cost, simple one-step synthesis, eliminating the need for additional passivation steps or the introduction of heteroatoms [25]. In this work, we developed a selective and sensitive nano-sensor using CDs (
<
2 nm) for detecting Cr(VI). The CDs demonstrate the ability to selectively detect Cr(VI) even under interference conditions from various metal ions. Additionally, the fluorescent sensor detects Cr(VI) over a concentration range of 1
–
1000 μM (linear range 1
–
125 μM, R2
≥
0.998), with a detection limit of 0.6 μM. The quenching mechanism is elucidated by the synergetic effect of the inner filter effect (IFE) with overlapping spectral bands in the range of 300–500 nm and the formation of a static quenching complex between CDs and Cr(VI). To evaluate the feasibility of this nano-sensor in real environments, the CDs were applied to detect micromolar amounts of Cr(VI) in drinking water and tap water samples.

Succinic acid (Sigma-Aldrich, 99.5 %) and Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Samchun Chemicals of Korea, 99 %) are used as precursors. All reagents of Q-Clx (M = Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Cd2+), R–Cl2·H2O (RNi2+, Mg2+, Cu2+), Pb(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, (CH3COO)2Cd·2H2O, Fe(CH3COO)2, CoSO4(H2O)7, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, and K2Cr2O7 are purchased from commercial suppliers (Sigma-Aldrich, Fluka, Junsei Chemical and Samchun Chemicals of KOREA) and are utilized without purification. The distilled ultrapure water is utilized to prepare all solutions.

The energy-filtering TEM images and high-resolution spherical aberration-corrected TEM (HR-Cs-TEM) were obtained using a LIBRA 120 (Carl Zeiss) and JEM-ARM200F (JEOL Ltd) system, respectively. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected using an X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker). The thermogram data for weight loss according to temperature were obtained by thermal gravity analysis (TGA, TGA-7, PerkinElmer). The elemental composition and chemical bindings were analyzed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, AXIS-HSi, KRATOS) and Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR, Cary 670, Agilent Technologies). The UV–Visible spectra were obtained using a UV–Vis. spectrometer (Cary 300, Agilent Technologies), and the fluorescence spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (FS-2, SCINCO). The fluorescence lifetime was evaluated using a Quantaurus-Tau system (C11367-31, Hamamatsu Photonics). The contents of Cr(VI) in real samples were quantified using inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, 5100, Agilent Technologies).

The modified method for synthesizing the CDs, based on a previously reported method [25], is described as follows: The 4 mmol of succinic acid and 4 mmol of tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane are added to 10 mL of distilled water. The mixture was sonicated for 20 min and then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was maintained at 200 °C for 15 h. After the reaction period, the autoclave is slowly cooled down and a yellowish suspension is obtained as a result. The obtained solution is neutralized using a KOH solution and then centrifugated at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove larger particles. The collected supernatant is further processed by lyophilization, resulting in the formation of a pale yellowish solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test of pH and stabilized time of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The CDs solution (4 mg/mL) using pH solutions in the range of approximate pH 2
–
12 adjusted with HCl or KOH are prepared to 5 mL, respectively. For fluorescence measurements, 2 mL of the CDs solution at different pH values are transferred into a quartz cuvette and the fluorescence spectra are recorded. To determine the equilibration time, the fluorescence intensities are monitored after adding Cr(VI) at a concentration of 125 μM. The measurements are taken at intervals of several minutes to observe any changes in fluorescence intensity over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Cr(VI) and interference cations
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution (ca. 0.2 mM) of potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7, purity 99.5 %) is prepared for a sensitivity analysis of Cr(VI). The 5 mg of K2Cr2O7 is dissolved in 100 mL standard volumetric flask. The fluorescence detection of the CDs solution with Cr(VI) is conduced as follows: The 2 mL of the CDs solution (4 mg/mL) is taken into the quartz cuvette and then an equivalent volume of Cr(VI) concentration is added stepwise by a micropipette. The mixed solution is left for 5 min and then the integrated fluorescence intensities at 350 nm of CDs’ Max. excitation are collected. To check the influence of interferences, the aqueous solutions of cation salts are also prepared for the stock solution at 1.0 mM and the procedure of fluorescent detection is followed described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Real field samples, including tap water and drinking water, were examined to evaluate the potential applicability of the synthesized CDs. Tap water sourced from our laboratory utility and commercially available drinking water were used. Prior to analysis, ICP-OES analysis was conducted to confirm blank samples before referring the amount of spiked Cr(VI). Three different concentrations of Cr(VI) were selected within the linear range of approximately 1–125 μM to observe fluorescence quenching at the micromole level.

As depicted in Scheme 1 , the synthesis of the CDs is carried out using succinic acid and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane as low-cost commercial sources through a facile one-step hydrothermal process [25]. The ratio of the reactants that yields the highest fluorescence intensity (quantum yields of 25.4 %, determined using a relative method, Fig. S1) is chosen for the development of the fluorescence sensor.

The shape and structure of the CDs were analyzed using TEM and HR-TEM. The as-prepared CDs exhibited an ultrafine particle morphology, with a size distribution of approximately 1.8
±
0.2 nm (Fig. 1 a and b). Furthermore, it was observed that the CDs exhibited a partially ordered lattice with a spacing of 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [26] and an amorphous region without any lattice ordering was also observed (Fig. 1c). The corresponding XRD pattern (Fig. 1d) further supported the findings. The XRD analysis revealed a semi-crystalline nature of the CDs, with a diffraction peak at 2 Theta = 19
°
, indicating the presence of a crystalline component and a broad peak at 2 Theta = 30
°
suggested the presence of an amorphous region [27,28]. The analysis indicates that the CDs are partially carbonized, as evidenced by the high organic content of 72.4 % (Fig. S2). This partial carbonization process can contribute to the hybrid properties of the CDs, which arise from a combination of the organic chain and carbon structure [29].

To investigate the elemental composition and chemical bonding in detail, XPS and FT-IR analyses were performed. The wide scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 a) indicates the presence of three elemental components corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, assigned at 285.3 eV, 399.3 eV, and 531.3 eV, respectively. The N1s peak at 399.3 eV is further deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 2b), which can be attributed to specific functional groups such as amine and amide (
−
N
−
C
=
O
−
,
−
NH
−
,
−
NH2) at 399.1 eV, pyridinic N/pyrrolic N at 400.1 eV, and quaternary N at 401.4 eV [21,30,31]. This indicates the presence of N-doped structures and related functional groups in the as-prepared CDs [25]. The deconvoluted peaks of the C1s and O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 2c and d) can be assigned as follows [21,30–32]: sp2 carbon (C
=
C/C
−
C) at 284.5 eV, sp3 carbon (C
−
N/C
−
O) at 285.4 eV, C
=
N at 287.5 eV, carbonyl groups (
−
O
−
C
=
O,
−
N
−
C
=
O) at 288.7 eV, OC at 530.3 eV, O
−
C at 531.7 eV, and O(H)–C at 533.3 eV. As observed in the FT-IR data of CDs (Fig. 2e), the characteristic IR peaks of each precursor do not overlap with those of the as-prepared CDs, except for peaks related to the alkyl sp3 carbon (C
−
H, 2937 & 2887 cm−1), ester C (C
=
O, 1695 cm−1), and primary alcohol (C
−
O, 1054 cm−1) bonds [21], which are present in the precursor molecules themselves. The FT-IR spectra of CDs provide evidence for the presence of amide groups, as indicated by the peaks at 1402 cm−1 (C
−
N), 1559 cm−1 (N
−
H), and the broad absorption in the range of 3000
−
3500 cm−1 (N–H, secondary amine) [31]. The peaks observed at 1054 cm−1 (C
−
O, primary alcohol), 1695 cm−1 (C
=
O), and the broad band 3199 cm−1 (O
−
H) can be attributed to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in the CDs [25]. These data provide evidence that the carbonization process occurs partially, as they indicate the presence of numerous functional groups and short organic chains in the CDs that have been preserved after carbonization [29]. The retention of these functional groups on the nanoparticles contributes to their hydrophilicity and ensures their stability under aqueous conditions [27,31]. These characteristics can be advantageous for the application of the CDs as a sensor, as they enable effective interaction with the surrounding aqueous environment. Fig. 2f shows the UV–vis absorbance and photoluminescent (PL) spectra of CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths. The as-prepared CDs exhibit an overall absorption in the UV range of 200
−
400 nm (Fig. 2f, left axis). The absorption spectrum exhibits a shoulder peak at 230 nm, which can be attributed to the π
−
π* transition of the conjugated bonds (C
=
C/C
=
N) [8,13]. Additionally, a broad peak at 300 nm is observed, which is associated with the n
―
π* transition originating from surface states containing functional groups such as amine, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups [8,13,29]. The PL spectra (Fig. 2f, right axis) are obtained by exciting CDs at a different wavelength. The as-synthesized CDs with their Max. emission at ca. 350 nm excitation display the characteristic property of excitation-dependent broad PL emission. This broad emission arises from the presence of various fluorescence centers and complex energy levels within the CDs [29].

The fluorescence (FL) stability of the CDs solution is evaluated at different pH values and equilibrium times. As shown in Fig. S3a, within the pH range of 2
−
12, the FL intensities of the CDs solution remain relatively stable with only slight decreases observed below pH 4 or above pH 8. This indicates that the CDs can be utilized effectively within a wide range of acidic and basic pH environments. In Fig. S3b, it is demonstrated that the equilibrium between the CDs and Cr(VI) is established within approximately 5 min after the addition of Cr(VI) ions.

To assess the selectivity of the CDs-based fluorescent sensor towards Cr(VI), fluorescence quenching experiments are performed using 1 mM concentrations of environmentally relevant cations including Na+, K+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Pb2+ and Cr(VI). The images of the CDs solutions (Fig. 3 a) are captured after the addition of various cations (Fig. 3a, upper) and after spiking Cr(VI) (Fig. 3a, lower) in the presence of other ions under 365 nm irradiation. It is observed that significant FL quenching occurs only with the addition of Cr(VI) ions, while the other cations exhibit relatively negligible influence at the same concentration. Furthermore, to examine the interference effect in the presence of other ions, 1 mM of Cr(VI) is individually added to the CDs solution along with 1 mM of other metal ions. The substantial FL quenching effect caused by the addition of Cr(VI) ions remains prominent even in the presence of other interfering ions. The graph in Fig. 3b illustrates the FL quenching ratio of 1 mM Cr(VI) as 93.7 %, while the same concentration of all other ions only leads to a quenching effect of less than 9.5 % at maximum. These results clearly indicate that the CD-based nanomaterials can serve as an efficient fluorescence sensor for the selective detection of Cr(VI) in various aqueous media, even in the presence of interference from other ions.

According to the mentioned above, the sensitivity and linear response of the CD-based Cr(VI) sensor were evaluated. In Fig. 4 a, the F0/F ratio of integrated fluorescence intensities shows an exponential increase within the range of 1
–
1000 μM of Cr(VI). This phenomenon has also been reported in several articles [7,17,20,33,34]. In the inset of Fig. 4a, the linear range of the sensor is determined to be 1
–
125 μM of Cr(VI), and the plot can be curve-fitted into the equation [F0/F] = 1.0612+Ksv[Cr(VI)], which is close to the well-known Stern−Volmer equation [33,35]. The S–V quenching constant (Ksv) is calculated to be 0.72
×
104 [L·mol−1] with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998. The detection of limit is determined to be 0.6 μM (31 μg L−1) using 3S/k where S represents the standard deviation of the blank and k is the slope of calibration plot [33]. This detection limit is lower than the WHO-approved Cr content in drinking water (50 μg L−1) [3,8]. Fig. 4b shows that the FL intensities of the CDs solutions, at the Max. excitation of 350 nm, gradually decrease as the concentration of Cr(VI) increases from 0 to 125 μM.

To investigate the mechanism of FL quenching in the CDs and Cr(VI) system, optical properties and fluorescence lifetime analysis were conducted. The excitation spectrum of the CDs showed a band around 350 nm, and the emission band under 350 nm excitation was centered at 420 nm (Fig. 5 a). Cr(VI) exhibited broad absorptions at approximately 260, 360, and 440 nm, which overlapped with the excitation and emission ranges of the CDs. This suggests that Cr(VI) ions can both absorb the excitation photons of the CDs and absorb the emitted FL from the CDs. Consequently, the absorbance properties of Cr(VI) lead to fluorescence quenching of the CDs, following the inner filter effect (IFE) in an efficient manner [36]. The average FL lifetime (τavg) of the CDs was calculated to be 10.4 ns, 10.7 ns, and 5.7 ns at 365 nm excitation for three samples (Fig. 5b). The FL lifetime of the CDs remained relatively unchanged within the linear range of Cr(VI) concentration, indicating that the FL quenching induced by Cr(VI) follows the IFE mechanism [36]. This phenomenon also involves the formation of non-radiative complexes between the CDs and the quencher at the ground state [7,33]. This observation is supported by the UV–Vis absorbance spectra (Fig. S4), where an enhanced absorption band below approximately 350 nm, related to the surface states of the CDs, is observed after the addition of Cr(VI). This can be attributed to the formation of CDs-Cr(VI) complexes [17,33]. This CDs-sensing system applied for Cr(VI) can be explained by the IFE combined with static quenching mechanism [33,36]. In addition, in the nonlinear range of high Cr(VI) concentration, the fluorescence lifetime decreases. Along with the nonlinear phenomenon observed in Fig. 4a, this can be attributed to a combination of self-absorption or dynamic quenching, which occurs due to energy transfer through inter- or intra-molecular interactions, rather than static quenching, at relatively high concentrations [7].

To evaluate the practicality of this nano-sensor in real environment, a recovery experiment was conducted by spiking a known amount of Cr(VI) into blank samples. The results, presented in Table 1 , indicate that Cr(VI) was not detected in the original samples and when using this CDs-based fluorescent sensor, the recoveries ranged from 91 % to 106 % (n = 3), which compares well with the values obtained using the ICP-OES method (n = 5), further confirming the reliability of the sensor. These results suggest that the CDs-based sensor is feasible and applicable for monitoring Cr(VI) in real water samples. Furthermore, the comparison of the as-synthesized CDs with other types of CDs used for Cr(VI) detection is summarized in Table 2 .
Even without the introduction of additional doping elements, these CDs exhibit performance comparable to previously reported N-CDs [9,44].

In conclusion, the highly fluorescent CDs were successfully prepared using a simple one-step hydrothermal process with low-cost commercial sources, without the need for additional heteroatoms. The obtained CDs demonstrated excellent selectivity for Cr(VI) even in the presence of interfering species, and they exhibited good sensitivity within a linear range of 1
–
125 μM (R2
≥
0.998) with a detection limit of 0.6 μM. The CDs were effectively used for the detection of trace amounts of Cr(VI) in drinking water and tap water samples, yielding acceptable recovery rates. This CD-based fluorescent sensor offers the advantages of simplicity, low cost, easy implementation, and excellent performance, making it a promising tool not only for detecting Cr(VI) in the environmental industry but also for the development of various environmental sensors.

Misun Hahn: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Myeongseok Jang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Youngseul Cho: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Minjun Bae: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Min Young Han: Resources, Formal analysis. Yuanzhe Piao: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2023R1A2C2007699 and 2022R1A6A1A0306303912) and the Nano Material Technology Development Program through the NRF funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT (NRF-2015M3A7B6027970). In addition, this research was partly supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) funded by the Korea government (MOTIE) (20215710100170).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2023.114642.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Regulations for chromium emissions to the aquatic environment in Europe and elsewhere
Chemosphere

Talanta

J. Hazard Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots derived from flax straw for highly sensitive and selective detections of cobalt, chromium, and ascorbic acid
